questions.q: In which year did the Royal Indian Navy Mutiny take place?
questions.explanation: The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny broke out in February 1946, a little over a year before independence.

questions.q: Which city was the main centre of the Royal Indian Navy Mutiny?
questions.explanation: The revolt began and was strongest at Bombay, though it spread to other ports as well.

questions.q: The participants in the 1946 mutiny belonged to which branch of the armed forces?
questions.explanation: It was a revolt by ratings (sailors) of the Royal Indian Navy.

questions.q: In which month of 1946 did the naval mutiny erupt?
questions.explanation: The mutiny began in February 1946, spreading rapidly within days.

questions.q: The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny is generally seen as a sign of what?
options: The weakening grip of colonial authority
The end of the world war
questions.explanation: The revolt signalled that colonial control over the armed services was crumbling.

questions.q: What rank of naval personnel led the 1946 mutiny?
questions.explanation: The mutiny was led by the lower ranks known as ratings, not by senior officers.

questions.q: Which shore establishment in Bombay was a key flashpoint of the mutiny?
questions.explanation: The signals training establishment HMIS Talwar was a major centre where the unrest began.

questions.q: Apart from political feeling, what immediate grievances sparked the mutiny?
questions.explanation: Sailors protested over poor service conditions, bad food, low pay and racial discrimination.

questions.q: The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny occurred while which world conflict had just ended?
questions.explanation: The mutiny came in early 1946, just months after the Second World War ended in 1945.

questions.q: The mutiny is also commonly referred to by what name?
questions.explanation: It is often called the Bombay Naval Revolt or the RIN Mutiny because Bombay was its epicentre.

questions.q: On which date in February 1946 did the ratings of HMIS Talwar begin their strike?
questions.explanation: The unrest at HMIS Talwar flared into a hunger strike and revolt on 18 February 1946.

questions.q: Who emerged as a leading figure of the Naval Central Strike Committee during the mutiny?
questions.explanation: Leading Signalman M. S. Khan was elected president of the Naval Central Strike Committee.

questions.q: Roughly how many naval ratings are estimated to have joined the 1946 mutiny across all ports?
questions.explanation: About 20,000 ratings on dozens of ships and shore establishments took part in the revolt.

questions.q: Which Congress leader played a prominent role in persuading the sailors to surrender?
questions.explanation: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel negotiated with the strikers and urged them to lay down their arms.

questions.q: On the ships and establishments, the ratings hoisted flags of which parties as a show of unity?
questions.explanation: Sailors flew the flags of the Congress, Muslim League and Communist Party together as a unity symbol.

questions.q: The naval mutiny was accompanied by sympathetic strikes and unrest in which other city?
questions.explanation: Karachi was another major centre, with the sloop HMIS Hindustan involved in a stand-off.

questions.q: What did the striking sailors do to their British naval ensigns during the revolt?
options: Pulled them down and raised nationalist flags
questions.explanation: Ratings hauled down the British ensigns and replaced them with the flags of nationalist parties.

questions.q: Which leader of the Communist-influenced student and labour movement supported the mutineers in Bombay?
questions.explanation: Aruna Asaf Ali and others on the left openly backed the sailors during the Bombay unrest.

questions.q: What was Mahatma Gandhi's general attitude toward the naval mutiny?
options: He called for an armed national revolt
He criticised the violence and indiscipline
questions.explanation: Gandhi disapproved of the mutiny's violence and felt it set an unhealthy example of indiscipline.

questions.q: The mutiny ended primarily because the ratings agreed to do what?
questions.explanation: The sailors surrendered after political leaders assured them their grievances would be considered without reprisals.

questions.q: During the Bombay disturbances linked to the mutiny, what tragic outcome occurred in the city?
options: Hundreds of civilians died in clashes and firing
The city was bombed from the air
questions.explanation: Street clashes and firing during the sympathetic civilian unrest in Bombay killed hundreds of people.

questions.q: Which British political development closely followed the naval mutiny in 1946?
options: The passing of the Rowlatt Act
The arrival of the Cabinet Mission
questions.explanation: The British Cabinet Mission reached India in March 1946, shortly after the mutiny, to discuss the transfer of power.

questions.q: Which incident at HMIS Talwar shortly before the strike helped inflame the ratings?
options: A ship sank in the harbour
Anti-British slogans were scrawled and a rating was punished
A new ship arrived from Britain
questions.explanation: Anti-British slogans appeared at Talwar and the harsh handling of rating B. C. Dutt over them fuelled anger.

questions.q: Rating B. C. Dutt, central to the Talwar incident, was associated with what underground group's name?
questions.explanation: B. C. Dutt and fellow ratings styled themselves the 'Azad Hindis,' echoing the spirit of Bose's movement.

questions.q: Which British admiral commanded the Royal Indian Navy and oversaw the suppression of the mutiny?
questions.explanation: Vice Admiral J. H. Godfrey, Flag Officer Commanding the Royal Indian Navy, directed the response and threatened force.

questions.q: Beyond Bombay and Karachi, the mutiny's ripples reached naval stations as far as which eastern port?
questions.explanation: Ratings at Calcutta and other establishments also joined in, making the revolt nearly nationwide among naval bases.

questions.q: The Muslim League leader who, like Patel, urged the ratings to give up the struggle was who?
questions.explanation: Muhammad Ali Jinnah also called on the Muslim ratings to surrender, paralleling Patel's appeal to the strikers.

questions.q: How many days did the active phase of the naval mutiny broadly last before the surrender?
questions.explanation: The revolt flared on 18 February and the ratings surrendered around 23 February 1946, lasting roughly five days.

questions.q: What lasting significance is often attributed to the 1946 naval mutiny by historians?
options: It convinced Britain it could no longer rely on Indian forces
It triggered the Second World War
questions.explanation: Many historians argue the mutiny showed Britain that the loyalty of Indian armed forces could no longer be assumed, hastening withdrawal.

questions.q: The ratings' decision to surrender, despite their strength, was largely shaped by what factor?
options: Lack of support from major political parties for continued revolt
questions.explanation: With Congress and League leaders urging restraint rather than armed continuation, the isolated ratings chose to surrender.
